meta.title: Insurance in India — A to Z
meta.description: A plain-language guide to what insurance is, its seven principles, the history of insurance in India and the world, the regulator IRDAI, the Indian market, who can sell insurance, and a directory of every licensed insurer — all in one place.
patchNotes: v1 (2026-07): Initial A-to-Z knowledge base published — what insurance is, seven principles, world & India history, IRDAI regulator, market snapshot, distribution channels, insurer directory (life 26 / general 26 / standalone health 7 / reinsurers 8), and glossary.
sources: IRDAI — Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (irdai.gov.in), including 'Evolution of Insurance' and the IRDAI Annual Report 2024-25
The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority Act, 1999
The Insurance Laws (Amendment) Act, 2021 (FDI raised to 74%)
Sabka Bima Sabki Raksha / Insurance Amendment, 2025 (FDI raised to 100%)
Economic Survey 2025-26 (premium, penetration, density, AUM figures)
Swiss Re Sigma 2025 (global insurance penetration comparison)
PIB / Press Information Bureau, Government of India (reform announcements)
The Hindu, Economic Times, ET BFSI, Business Standard (market and reform reporting, 2025-26)
UK Insurance Act 2015 (proximate cause and disclosure principles)
Code of Hammurabi and Trennery, 'The Historical Development of Marine Insurance in England' (global history)
Wikipedia — 'List of insurance companies in India' and 'History of insurance' (compiled for cross-checking; always verify the live list on IRDAI)
Cyril Amarchand Mangaldas — distribution and intermediary guide 2026
iPleaders, Skillcast, SLM/MBA notes (explanation of the seven principles)

sourceMap.PIB: PIB / Press Information Bureau, Govt. of India

updateMechanism.howToPatch: This file is versioned. To fix a mistake or add an update: bump 'meta.version' by 1, set 'meta.lastUpdated' to the current YYYY-MM, add a short note to 'meta.patchNotes', then re-run 'node scripts/build.mjs'. The guide page reads this file at load time, so changes appear automatically after a rebuild and cache refresh.
updateMechanism.verifyLiveData: Insurer counts and lists change. Always confirm the current licensed list at irdai.gov.in before relying on it. A 'sourceNote' is attached to the insurers section for this reason.

whatIsInsurance.definition: Insurance is a simple idea with a big name. Many people put a little money into one common pool. When one of them suffers a loss — a fire, an accident, a hospital bill — the pool pays for it. You are not betting against yourself. You are sharing risk with strangers so that no single person is crushed by a bad day.

buildingBlocks.meaning: The person or business that buys the cover. That is you.
The company that runs the pool and pays valid claims.
The amount you pay, usually yearly, to stay in the pool.
The amount the insurer pays when a valid claim happens.
The event that is covered — fire, death, theft, accident, illness.
How the insurer decides whether to cover you, and at what price.

whyUsed.example: A ₹5 lakh hospital bill does not wipe out your family's savings.
You sleep easy knowing a rainy day is already planned for.
A term plan pays your family if something happens to you; an endowment plan also matures and pays you.
Banks lend safely, businesses run, and a family survives the loss of its main earner.

whatIsInsurance.commonMyth: Many people think insurance is 'a waste if nothing happens'. The truth: the peace of mind and the safety net are the product. You hope you never need it — that is the point.

principles.name: Utmost Good Faith (Uberrima Fides)
principles.plainDefinition: Both sides must be honest and tell each other everything that matters. The buyer must share facts that affect the risk. The seller must explain the cover clearly.
principles.example: You buy health insurance but hide that you already have diabetes. Later you claim for a diabetes-related treatment. The insurer can reject the claim and cancel the policy from day one.
principles.indiaNuance: In India, if you hide a material fact, the insurer can void the policy even years later — the duty of disclosure is strict. It does not help to say 'the agent filled the form', because the proposal is your responsibility.
principles.commonMyth: 'The agent ticked the wrong box, so it is not my fault.' It usually is — you sign the proposal.

principles.name: Insurable Interest
principles.plainDefinition: You can only insure something you would lose money from if it were damaged or lost.
principles.example: You can insure your own car. You cannot insure your neighbour's car, because you would not lose anything if it crashed.
principles.indiaNuance: In life insurance the interest must exist when you buy the policy. In fire and general insurance it must exist at the time of the loss. In marine insurance it must exist at the time of loss too.
principles.commonMyth: 'I can take a policy on anyone.' No — you need a genuine financial connection (like a spouse, dependent child, or business partner).

principles.name: Indemnity
principles.plainDefinition: Insurance is meant to put you back where you were — not to make you richer. You get paid for the loss, not for profit.
principles.example: Your car is worth ₹4 lakh. After an accident the insurer pays up to ₹4 lakh (minus deductions), not ₹10 lakh.
principles.indiaNuance: Life insurance is the big exception. You cannot put a price on a life, so a life policy pays the agreed sum assured regardless of 'loss'. Motor and health policies mostly follow indemnity.
principles.commonMyth: 'My insurance will make me money.' Indemnity rules are designed to stop exactly that.

principles.name: Subrogation
principles.plainDefinition: After the insurer pays your claim, it steps into your shoes and tries to recover the money from the person who actually caused the loss.
principles.example: Another driver dents your parked car. Your insurer pays your repair bill, then claims that amount from the other driver's insurer.
principles.indiaNuance: You cannot claim the same loss twice. Once you are paid, the right to recover from the wrongdoer passes to your insurer.
principles.commonMyth: 'I got paid, so I can also sue the other guy for the same amount.' You cannot double-recover.

principles.name: Contribution
principles.plainDefinition: If you have two policies covering the same risk, the insurers share the payout. You cannot claim the full amount from both.
principles.example: You have two motor policies on one car. After a claim, both insurers pay a proportion so that together you get no more than the loss.
principles.indiaNuance: This works hand-in-hand with indemnity — the aim is to compensate, never to enrich.
principles.commonMyth: 'More policies means more money.' Not for the same risk — they only share the bill.

principles.name: Proximate Cause (Causa Proxima)
principles.plainDefinition: When several things happen, the claim is decided by the main, closest cause — not by the last small event.
principles.example: A rider falls off a horse and hits his head. He later catches pneumonia and dies. The fall (the accident) is the proximate cause, so the accidental-death claim stands — subject to the policy wording.
principles.indiaNuance: Courts look for the 'dominant' cause. If the dominant cause is covered but the last event is not, the claim is usually still payable.
principles.commonMyth: 'The last thing that happened decides the claim.' Wrong — it is the dominant cause that matters.

principles.name: Loss Minimization (Mitigation)
principles.plainDefinition: If something goes wrong, you must try to limit the damage. You cannot just stand by and let the loss grow.
principles.example: Your kitchen catches fire. You call the fire brigade and try to save what you can. You do not simply watch it burn while thinking 'the insurer will pay'.
principles.indiaNuance: The policy requires you to take reasonable steps to reduce the loss. Failing to do so can reduce or void the claim.
principles.commonMyth: 'Once it is insured, I need do nothing.' You still have a duty to act sensibly.

historyGlobal.detail: Babylonian law allowed 'bottomry' — a loan for a sea voyage where the debt was cancelled if the ship was lost. Risk was baked into the interest. One of the earliest traces of insurance thinking.
Greek 'foenus nauticum' and Rhodian law introduced 'general average' — if cargo was thrown overboard to save a ship, all merchants shared the loss. The idea of sharing risk is already there.
Romans formed 'collegia funeraticia' that paid for a member's funeral and gave the family a small payout. An early form of life cover for ordinary people.
Craft guilds supported members who were sick, widowed, or burned out. Mutual help, not profit.
In Genoa and other Italian ports, written marine insurance contracts appear. Risk is now a separate, tradable thing.
Edward Lloyd's London coffee house becomes the meeting place for ship owners, merchants, and those willing to back their voyages. This grows into the famous Lloyd's market.
A fire destroys much of the city. Nicholas Barbon sets up one of the first fire insurance offices soon after, because people realise homes need protecting.
The first life insurer open to the public is formed in London.
The Equitable Society introduces proper actuarial pricing — premiums based on real odds of death. Modern life insurance is born.
English common-law cases settle the seven principles we still use — good faith, insurable interest, indemnity, subrogation, contribution, proximate cause, loss minimization.
The UK modernises insurance law, clarifying disclosure duties and remedies for non-disclosure. India's principles trace back to this same common-law tradition.

historyIndia.detail: The first life insurer in India, started in Calcutta. It mainly served Europeans and struggled, closing in 1834 — but it begins the story.
The first general (non-life) insurer, also in Calcutta, covering fire and marine risks.
The first Indian-owned life insurer. Notably, it charged Indians and Europeans the same premium at a time when others did not.
The first all-classes Indian general insurer, covering life, fire, and accident.
The Indian Life Assurance Companies Act brings early regulation of life business.
The Indian Insurance Companies Act starts collecting data on both life and non-life insurers.
A single umbrella law for the whole industry. It is still the parent law today, amended many times since.
The Life Insurance Corporation Act merges about 245 life insurers into one state-owned LIC. A near-monopoly for decades.

The General Insurance Business (Nationalisation) Act brings about 107 general insurers under state control, grouped into four public-sector companies — National, New India, Oriental, and United — with GIC as the sole reinsurer.
A committee recommends opening the sector to private players and setting up an independent regulator. This reshapes modern Indian insurance.
The IRDA Act, 1999 sets up the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority. Private insurers enter; foreign direct investment (FDI) is allowed at 26%.
The sector is opened further to foreign partners.
The Insurance Laws (Amendment) Act, 2021 allows up to 74% foreign ownership. (Note: 100% FDI did NOT happen in 2021 — a common mix-up.)
Indian insurers move to Ind AS 117, the local version of the global IFRS 17 accounting standard, from April 2026 — changing how insurers report profits.

New amendments allow 100% FDI and launch three big public initiatives — Bima Sugam (a one-stop digital marketplace), Bima Vahak (women-led last-mile reach), and Bima Vistaar (simplified rural cover). The mission: 'Insurance for All by 2047'.

regulator.name: IRDAI — Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India
regulator.formed: IRDAI was created by the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority Act, 1999, and began functioning in 2000. Its head office is in Hyderabad. It took over the regulatory role earlier held by the Controller of Insurance (under the Ministry of Finance).
regulator.role: Think of IRDAI as the referee of the insurance market. Before IRDAI, the sector was small and mostly state-run. After private players were allowed in from 2000, the country needed an independent body to keep things fair. IRDAI's main jobs are: licensing insurers, agents, brokers and other intermediaries; protecting policyholders (for example through the Bima Bharosa grievance system); approving products so they are clear and fair; checking that claims are paid quickly; promoting competition and fair pricing; and pushing financial inclusion so more Indians are covered. It also sets accounting and solvency rules so that insurers stay financially strong and can pay claims even in a bad year.
regulator.whoReportsTo: Ministry of Finance, Government of India.
regulator.commonMyth: 'IRDAI sells insurance.' No — it only regulates. You buy from insurers, not from the regulator.

market.premium: Total premium collected was about ₹11.9 lakh crore in FY25, with assets under management around ₹74 lakh crore (Economic Survey 2025-26).
market.penetration: Insurance penetration — premium as a share of GDP — is about 3.7% (life around 2.7%, non-life around 1%). That is roughly half the global average of about 7% (Swiss Re).
market.density: Insurance density — premium per person — is about $97, far below the global figure near $900.
market.trend: Health insurance has overtaken motor as the largest part of the non-life market (about 41% of general domestic premium), reflecting rising medical costs and awareness.
market.protectionGap: India still has a large 'protection gap' — many households are under-insured, so a single hospital bill or death can be financially devastating.

governmentSchemes.name: Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana (PMJJBY)
governmentSchemes.detail: Life cover of ₹2 lakh for an annual premium of about ₹330.

governmentSchemes.name: Pradhan Mantri Suraksha Bima Yojana (PMSBY)
governmentSchemes.detail: Accident cover of ₹2 lakh for about ₹12 a year.

governmentSchemes.name: Ayushman Bharat / PM-JAY
governmentSchemes.detail: Health cover up to ₹5 lakh per family per year for eligible low-income households.

governmentSchemes.name: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY)
governmentSchemes.detail: Crop insurance that protects farmers against yield loss.

distribution.title: Who can sell you insurance in India

channels.name: Agent
channels.detail: A person authorised to sell the products of ONE insurer. The classic LIC or private agent.

channels.name: Corporate Agent
channels.detail: A company (often a bank) authorised to sell policies of one or a few insurers. Bancassurance is this model — you buy at the bank counter.

channels.name: Insurance Broker
channels.detail: Works for YOU, not the insurer. Licensed by IRDAI (well over a thousand brokers are licensed). A broker can place your risk across many insurers and advise on the best fit.

channels.name: Web Aggregator
channels.detail: Online platforms (such as Policybazaar) that list and compare policies from several insurers.

channels.name: Insurance Marketing Firm
channels.detail: A firm that can offer products of multiple insurers plus related services, reaching more customers than a single-agent model.

channels.name: Point of Sale (POS) person
channels.detail: A trained person who sells simple, standard products and helps with servicing.

distribution.tip: If you want unbiased advice across insurers, a broker or aggregator is closest to 'working for you'. An agent or bank usually represents one insurer.

insurers.sourceNote: This is a verified snapshot as of 2026-07. Insurer counts and licences change, and new standalone health insurers have been added in 2025-26. Always confirm the live, official list at irdai.gov.in before buying or relying on this data. Figures here are stable facts (name, sector, base, founded, website) only — not yearly metrics like claim-settlement ratios, which you should check on IRDAI.

life.name: Life Insurance Corporation of India (LIC)
life.notes: The country's largest life insurer; created by nationalising ~245 insurers.

life.name: Axis Max Life
life.notes: Private life insurer (Max Life). (Verify current branding.)

life.name: SBI Life
life.notes: Private life insurer promoted by State Bank of India.

life.name: Generali Central Life
life.notes: Private life insurer (Future Generali / Central rebranded — verify current name).

life.name: Bandhan Life
life.notes: Private life insurer (formerly Aegon Life — verify current branding).

life.name: Credit Access Life
life.notes: Newer private life insurer (verify licence status).

general.name: New India Assurance
general.notes: One of the four public-sector general insurers.

general.name: Royal Sundaram General
general.notes: First private general insurer after liberalisation.

general.name: Agriculture Insurance Company of India (AIC)
general.notes: Implements crop insurance schemes like PMFBY.

general.name: Export Credit Guarantee Corporation (ECGC)
general.notes: Covers exporters against non-payment by overseas buyers.

general.name: ManipalCigna General
general.notes: Private general insurer; also operates a standalone health arm.

healthStandalone.name: ManipalCigna Health Insurance
healthStandalone.notes: Standalone health arm of the ManCigna group.

healthStandalone.name: Galaxy Health Insurance
healthStandalone.notes: Newer standalone health insurer (verify licence status).

healthStandalone.name: Narayana Health Insurance
healthStandalone.notes: Newest standalone health entrant (verify licence status).

reinsurers.name: General Insurance Corporation of India (GIC Re)
reinsurers.notes: India's national reinsurer; all insurers cede a share of business to it by regulation.

reinsurers.name: Lloyd's India
reinsurers.notes: Indian branch of the Lloyd's insurance market.

glossary.meaning: A maths expert who works out the odds and prices premiums fairly.
Transferring your policy rights to someone else (for example, as loan collateral).
The person who receives the money when a claim is paid.
The part of risk an insurer passes to a reinsurer.
Share of claims an insurer pays versus receives. A trust signal — check it on IRDAI before buying.
A life policy that pays on death OR on maturity, whichever comes first.
Things the policy will NOT cover. Read these carefully.
An old sea rule: if cargo is sacrificed to save a ship, all shippers share the loss.
A period (often 5 years) after which a policy cannot be challenged for non-disclosure, if premiums were paid.
The risk that being insured makes someone careless or dishonest.
A discount on next year's premium because you made no claim.
The specific event covered — fire, flood, accident, death.
The price you pay to keep the cover.
An extra benefit added to a base policy (for example, critical illness cover).
The amount paid on a valid claim.
A company that handles cashless hospital claims on behalf of the insurer.
The insurer's process of deciding whether to cover you and at what price.
The duty of both sides to be fully honest.
Time you must wait after buying before certain cover starts (common in health insurance).
The return you get from a savings-linked insurance plan.
